courses.name: Hebrew
courses.tagline: From your very first alef — letters, words & street slang
courses.taglineEs: Desde tu primera álef — letras, palabras y jerga callejera

letters.name: Alef
letters.mnemonic: A silent ninja — you see it, you never hear it!

letters.name: Bet
letters.mnemonic: Bet looks like a house with an open door — 'bayit' means house!

letters.name: Gimel
letters.mnemonic: Gimel is a giraffe galloping on one long leg.

letters.name: Dalet
letters.mnemonic: Dalet is a door frame — 'delet' means door!

letters.name: Hey
letters.mnemonic: Hey! It's waving at you with a little gap to breathe through.

letters.name: Vav
letters.mnemonic: Vav is a hook — the skinniest letter in the club.

letters.name: Zayin
letters.mnemonic: Zayin is a sword — zzzing!

letters.name: Chet
letters.mnemonic: Chet is the sound of clearing your throat. Practice politely!

letters.name: Tet
letters.mnemonic: Tet curls up like a snake in a basket.

letters.name: Yod
letters.mnemonic: Yod is tiny but mighty — the smallest letter of all!

letters.name: Kaf
letters.mnemonic: Kaf is the palm of your hand, curved and ready to catch.

letters.name: Lamed
letters.mnemonic: Lamed is the tallest letter — a lighthouse above the line!

letters.name: Mem
letters.mnemonic: Mem ripples like a wave — 'mayim' means water!

letters.name: Nun
letters.mnemonic: Nun is a little fish swimming down the line.

letters.name: Samech
letters.mnemonic: Samech is a circle of support — it has your back all around.

letters.name: Ayin
letters.mnemonic: Ayin means 'eye' — it watches silently from the back of your throat.

letters.name: Pey
letters.mnemonic: Pey is a mouth mid-sentence — 'peh' means mouth!

letters.name: Tsadi
letters.mnemonic: Tsadi buzzes like 'pizza' — tsss!

letters.name: Kof
letters.mnemonic: Kof is a monkey with its tail hanging below the line.

letters.name: Resh
letters.mnemonic: Resh is a head in profile — 'rosh' means head!

letters.name: Shin
letters.mnemonic: Shin has three flames — shhh, the fire is sleeping.

letters.name: Tav
letters.mnemonic: Tav is the grand finale — last letter, big foot, takes a bow!

expressions.english: what do you do (for a living)?

courses.name: English
courses.tagline: 5,600 words and idioms — from everyday to advanced English
courses.taglineEs: 5.600 palabras y modismos — del inglés cotidiano al avanzado

words.hebrew: the money you're paid each month for your job
words.example: My salary is paid on the first of the month.
She has an extra income from renting a room.

words.hebrew: a meeting where you answer questions to get a job
words.example: I have a job interview tomorrow morning.

words.hebrew: the last moment to finish something
words.example: The deadline for the report is Friday.

words.hebrew: to discuss something to reach an agreement
words.example: We negotiated a better price for the car.

words.hebrew: moving up to a higher position at work
words.example: He got a promotion after two years.

words.hebrew: knowledge and skill you get from doing things
words.example: She has five years of experience in sales.

words.hebrew: a chance to do something good
words.example: This job is a great opportunity for you.

words.hebrew: sad because something didn't happen as you hoped
words.example: I was disappointed by the film's ending.

words.hebrew: uncomfortable and ashamed in front of others
words.example: He was embarrassed when he forgot her name.

words.hebrew: feeling good about something you or others did
words.example: She's proud of her exam results.

words.hebrew: unhappy because someone has what you want
words.example: Don't be jealous of your friend's new phone.

words.hebrew: worried and nervous about what may happen
words.example: I always feel anxious before exams.

words.hebrew: sure about yourself and your abilities
words.example: Try to look confident at the interview.

words.hebrew: wanting to know and learn new things
words.example: Children are curious about everything.
I'm really grateful for your help.

words.hebrew: a person who lives next to you
words.example: Our neighbour waters our plants in summer.
Don't open the door to strangers.

words.hebrew: a large group of people in one place
words.example: A crowd gathered in the main square.

words.hebrew: the group of people who run a country
words.example: The government announced new taxes.

words.hebrew: all the people in a country, seen as one group
words.example: Sport plays a big role in our society.

words.hebrew: people who share a place or an interest
words.example: Our community organises a street market.

words.hebrew: all the people born around the same time
words.example: My generation grew up with the internet.

words.hebrew: an official member of a country
words.example: She became a citizen last year.

words.hebrew: travelling from one place to another
words.example: The journey to the coast took six hours.

words.hebrew: the place you are travelling to
words.example: We reached our destination at noon.

words.hebrew: the bags you take when you travel
words.example: My luggage was too heavy for the flight.

words.hebrew: when something happens later than planned
words.example: There's a two-hour delay on all trains.

words.hebrew: a place where two roads cross
words.example: Turn left at the crossroads.

words.hebrew: the path beside the road where you walk
words.example: Keep the bikes off the pavement, please.

words.hebrew: a list of the times buses or trains leave
words.example: Check the timetable before you leave.

words.hebrew: in or to a foreign country
words.example: She's been working abroad since May.

words.hebrew: to succeed in reaching a goal
words.example: He achieved everything he planned this year.
Try to avoid sugary drinks.
Daily practice improves your English fast.

words.hebrew: to make someone agree by giving reasons
words.example: She persuaded me to join the gym.

words.hebrew: to move something to a later time
words.example: They postponed the match because of rain.
He refused the offer politely.

words.hebrew: to get the result you wanted
words.example: You'll succeed if you keep trying.

words.hebrew: to tell someone that something is good and worth trying
words.example: I recommend the fish — it's excellent here.

words.hebrew: a temporary marker word for live-refresh verification
words.example: This is a ZZZ Live Refresh Test sentence.

idioms.hebrew: to make people feel relaxed when they first meet
idioms.example: A funny story is a good way to break the ice.
The test was a piece of cake.
I'm exhausted — time to hit the sack.
I'm staying home — I feel under the weather.
We eat out once in a blue moon.

idioms.english: cost an arm and a leg
idioms.example: That new phone costs an arm and a leg.
In a nutshell, we need more time.

idioms.hebrew: to solve two problems with one action
idioms.english: kill two birds with one stone
idioms.example: Cycling to work kills two birds with one stone.
I'm still on the fence about the new job.
She was over the moon about the news.

idioms.hebrew: suddenly too nervous to do something planned
idioms.example: He got cold feet just before the speech.
We don't always see eye to eye, but we're friends.
Can you give me a hand with these boxes?
That noise is getting on my nerves.

idioms.hebrew: to watch someone or something carefully
idioms.example: Keep an eye on my bag for a minute, please.

idioms.hebrew: said when the person you were talking about appears
idioms.example: Speak of the devil — we were just talking about you!

courses.name: French
courses.tagline: Already conversational? Enrich your vocabulary — B2-C1

words.hebrew: qui parle avec aisance et convainc
words.example: Son discours était si éloquent que tout le monde a applaudi.
Ne t'inquiète pas, c'était une remarque anodine.
Quelle idée saugrenue de partir à minuit !

words.hebrew: ni vraiment positif ni vraiment négatif
words.example: Les critiques du film sont mitigées.
Ses explications restent floues.
Ce dessert est absolument exquis.
Un dimanche morne et pluvieux.
Il a répondu d'un ton désinvolte.
On a flâné le long de la Seine.
J'ai déniché ce livre dans une brocante.
Il a esquivé la question du journaliste.
Il râle dès qu'il pleut.
Elle se débrouille très bien en français.
Je peaufine ma présentation ce soir.
Ne bâcle pas tes devoirs !
Nous avons entamé les négociations.

words.hebrew: heureux et bien dans sa vie
words.example: Elle semble très épanouie dans son nouveau travail.
Je suis agacé par ce bruit constant.
Il était bouleversé par la nouvelle.

words.hebrew: qui ne fait pas facilement confiance
words.example: Sois méfiant avec les offres trop belles.
Les enfants étaient émerveillés par le feu d'artifice.
Après l'échec, il était complètement abattu.
Je suis soulagé que tout se soit bien passé.
Il est revenu tout penaud s'excuser.

words.hebrew: ce qu'on peut gagner ou perdre
words.example: L'enjeu de cette réunion est énorme.

words.hebrew: série d'actions pour obtenir quelque chose
words.example: Les démarches administratives prennent du temps.
Voici une ébauche de mon projet.

words.hebrew: ce qui manque, un vide dans les connaissances
words.example: J'ai des lacunes en grammaire.

words.hebrew: capable de faire plusieurs choses différentes
words.example: On cherche quelqu'un de polyvalent.
Un pilote chevronné a repris les commandes.
Cette filière offre de nombreux débouchés.
Parler trois langues est un vrai atout.
Faire la vaisselle, quelle corvée !
Ce vol à trente euros, quelle aubaine !
Un imprévu m'empêche de venir.
Quel gaspillage de jeter tout ce pain !
Je suis coincé dans les embouteillages.

words.hebrew: sensation agréable de changement de décor
words.example: Ce voyage au Japon, quel dépaysement !

words.hebrew: ce qui arrive tous les jours, la routine
words.example: La lecture fait partie de mon quotidien.

words.hebrew: petits travaux manuels à la maison
words.example: Il passe son dimanche à faire du bricolage.

idioms.hebrew: ne pas venir à un rendez-vous
idioms.example: Il m'a posé un lapin hier soir !
Depuis son départ, j'ai le cafard.

idioms.english: coûter les yeux de la tête
idioms.example: Cette veste coûte les yeux de la tête.
Le dimanche, je fais la grasse matinée.

idioms.hebrew: ne pas avoir envie de faire un effort
idioms.example: J'ai la flemme de cuisiner ce soir.

idioms.hebrew: ne pas dormir de la nuit
idioms.example: J'ai passé une nuit blanche avant l'examen.
Tu m'écoutes ? Tu es dans la lune !

idioms.hebrew: abandonner parce qu'on ne trouve pas la réponse
idioms.example: Je ne sais pas… je donne ma langue au chat !
Entre eux, ça a été le coup de foudre.
Il fait si chaud qu'elle est tombée dans les pommes.

idioms.hebrew: donner son avis sans qu'on le demande
idioms.example: Il faut toujours qu'il mette son grain de sel !
Arrête de chercher midi à quatorze heures !

idioms.hebrew: avoir des choses plus importantes à faire
idioms.example: Je n'ai pas le temps, j'ai d'autres chats à fouetter.
Après trois essais, il a jeté l'éponge.

idioms.english: mettre les pieds dans le plat
idioms.example: Il a mis les pieds dans le plat en parlant du divorce.
Pour ce projet, toute l'équipe est au taquet.
